meta.title: R172 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes SLK R172 engine — M271, M274, M276, M152, OM651. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the last V8 SLK explained. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes SLK R172 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned R172 engine typically costs £2,500–£12,000+ supply only, depending on the engine — the M271/M274 four-cylinders sit at £2,500–£5,500, while the M152 V8 in the SLK 55 AMG ranges from £7,500–£12,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes R172 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M274.920 four-cylinder (SLC 200/SLC 300, 2015–2020) and the OM651.980 diesel (SLK 250 CDI/SLC 250 d) are the most dependable. The M274 is a significant improvement over the M271 — more reliable, more responsive, and more efficient. The OM651 is a proven, robust diesel with a strong reputation for longevity. The M276 V6 (SLK 350) is also well-regarded, though timing chain wear affects some examples.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes SLK R172?

acceptedAnswer.text: M271 timing chain and camshaft adjuster failure (SLK 200/250, pre-facelift), M276 V6 timing chain wear (SLK 350), OM651 injector seal failure (diesel), Vario-roof mechanism failure (all models), M152 V8 camshaft adjuster wear (SLK 55 AMG), and — unrelated to the engine — electrical issues with the retractable hardtop.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes SLK R172 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and the year. The M274 four-cylinder (2015–2020 facelift) and OM651 diesel are genuinely robust. The M271 pre-facelift four-cylinder has known timing-chain issues — budget for inspection. The M276 V6 is well-regarded but needs timing chain checks. The M152 V8 is reliable for a high-performance engine but expensive to maintain. The Vario-roof mechanism is the single biggest non-engine reliability concern.

mainEntity.name: Which R172 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but pre-facelift M271.860/861 (SLK 200/SLK 250, 2011–2015) needs careful checking for timing chain history. If you're buying one, listen for a rattling noise on cold start — this is the classic early warning sign. The M152 V8 (SLK 55 AMG) is a specialist engine — if you can afford a £15,000+ car, you can afford the maintenance, but it's not for the faint-hearted.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my R172 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain rattle on cold start (M271, M276, M152), rough idle or diesel smell (OM651 injector failure), or roof mechanism warning lights or failure to operate are key warnings. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes SLK R172 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: The R172 is a premium roadster with strong enthusiast values — a £3,000–£4,500 OM651 recon against a £8,000 SLK 250 CDI is 38–56% of the car's value, safely below our 70% Mercedes threshold. Even the M271 at £2,500–£4,500 against a £7,000 SLK 200 is 36–64% — within the threshold. The only exceptions are the AMG models — but if you can afford a £15,000+ SLK 55 AMG or SLC 43, you can afford the maintenance. For the M152 V8, the maths is different — it's the last naturally aspirated V8 SLK, a collector's item with collector's bills.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes SLK R172 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M274 four-cylinder and OM651 diesel can exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing. The M271 is typically good for 120,000+ miles if the timing chain has been addressed. The M276 V6 is good for 150,000+ miles if maintained. The M152 V8 is typically good for 80,000–120,000 miles if maintained to AMG standards, but running costs are significantly higher.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the SLK and SLC?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are the R172 generation. The pre-facelift model (2011–2015) was badged SLK, while the facelift model (2015–2020) was rebadged SLC. The facelift brought new engines (M274 replacing M271), the 9G-Tronic automatic transmission, revised styling, and updated technology. They share the same platform, chassis, and most components — the difference is primarily engines, transmission, and styling. This page covers both variants.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes SLK R172 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., SLK 200, SLK 250 CDI, SLC 300) and registration year (pre-2015 or post-2015), and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes SLK R172 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the R172 roadster — from the M271 1.8-litre turbo fours to the M274 2.0-litre replacements, the OM651 diesel, the M276 V6 in the SLK 350 and AMG SLC 43, and the naturally aspirated M152 V8 in the SLK 55 AMG. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the engine changes that came with the 2015 SLC facelift. Backed by real UK R172 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes SLK R172 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the R172 roadster specifically — spanning both pre-facelift SLK (2011–2015) and facelift SLC (2015–2020) variants.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: M276 V6 (SLK 350): 85% confidence — a well-regarded naturally aspirated V6 with good reliability, though timing chain issues affect some examples. M152 V8: 85% confidence — one of Mercedes' finest naturally aspirated V8s, exclusive to the SLK 55 AMG, with cylinder deactivation for efficiency. OM651 diesel: 80% confidence — proven four-cylinder diesel with strong longevity. M274 four-cylinder: 80% confidence — the 2.0-litre turbo that replaced the M271, generally more reliable. M271: 75% confidence — known for timing chain and camshaft adjuster issues. M276 twin-turbo (AMG SLC 43): 75% confidence — high-performance twin-turbo V6 with good power but more complex.

overview.intro: The R172 is the third-generation SLK-Class, launched in January 2011 at the Geneva Motor Show as the successor to the R171. Built in Bremen, Germany, it features a retractable hardtop, a 2,484 mm wheelbase, and a front-engine, rear-wheel-drive layout. The engine range spans the M271 1.8-litre turbo four-cylinder (2011–2015), the M274 2.0-litre turbo four-cylinder (2015–2020 facelift), the OM651 2.1-litre diesel four-cylinder (2012–2020), the M276 3.5-litre V6 (SLK 350) and 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 (AMG SLC 43), and the M152 5.5-litre naturally aspirated V8 in the SLK 55 AMG. A 2015 facelift brought new engines, the 9G-Tronic automatic transmission, and the SLC name. The R172 was the first SLK to offer a diesel engine and the last to feature a naturally aspirated V8.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes R172 (shared with W204 C-Class) • Predecessor: R171 • Successor: — (discontinued after 2020) • Sibling platform: W204 C-Class.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The R172 SLK/SLC generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the M271/M274 four-cylinders and OM651 diesel as these cars enter enthusiast-owner territory.

items.quote: The 3.5-litre M276 V6 delivers 306 PS of naturally aspirated power — smooth, responsive, and with a sound that no turbocharged four can match. It's the R172's sweet spot: V6 refinement without the M152's running costs, and more reliable than the M271 four-cylinder. The M276 is a well-regarded engine with good reliability, though timing chain issues affect some examples.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 smoothness and the classic roadster experience without AMG bills.

items.quote: The 1.8-litre M271 turbo four is a great engine — but it comes with known issues: timing chain stretch, camshaft adjuster wear, and oil leaks from the camshaft solenoid. The M274 that replaced it in the 2015 facelift is a much safer bet. If you're buying a pre-facelift SLK 200 or SLK 250, budget for timing-chain inspection.
items.whoItsFor: buyers willing to accept higher maintenance costs for the pre-facelift experience.

items.quote: The 5.5-litre M152 V8 is a masterpiece — 422 PS of naturally aspirated AMG thunder, exclusive to the SLK 55 AMG. It features cylinder deactivation derived from Formula 1 technology and weighs just 187 kg. But when it fails, it fails expensively. V8-specific parts, AMG labour rates, and the sheer complexity of this engine mean replacement bills frequently hit five figures. It's the last naturally aspirated V8 SLK — treat it as the special car it is.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts with deep pockets who want the last naturally aspirated V8 SLK.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre M274 turbo four is a significant improvement over the M271 — 184 PS in the SLC 200 and 245 PS in the SLC 300, with 300 Nm and 370 Nm of torque respectively. It's more reliable, more responsive, and more efficient than the engine it replaced. The SLC 300 is the sweet spot — 245 PS in a lightweight roadster is more than enough for UK roads.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best combination of performance, reliability, and value in the R172 range.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 in the AMG SLC 43 produces 367–390 PS — more than the SLK 55 AMG's 422 PS? No, but close, and with significantly better fuel economy. It replaced the M152 V8 in the facelift range and offers AMG performance without the V8's running costs. Often overlooked in favour of the V8, the SLC 43 is the smarter daily-driver choice.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want AMG performance with V6 running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Highest Risk — great engine, but check timing chain.
Best Petrol — smooth V6, classic roadster experience.
AMG V6 — fast, but more complex than the V8? No, simpler.
Most Expensive — last naturally aspirated V8 SLK.

economicsRule.title: The R172 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£4,500 OM651 recon against a £8,000 R172 sits at 38–56% of the car's value — safe. Even the M271 at £2,500–£4,500 against a £7,000 SLK 200 is 36–64% — within our 70% Mercedes threshold but approaching it on lower-value examples. The R172 is a premium roadster with strong enthusiast values; engine replacement is generally viable. The only exceptions are the AMG models — but if you can afford a £15,000+ SLK 55 AMG or SLC 43, you can afford the maintenance. For the M152 V8, the maths is different — it's the last naturally aspirated V8 SLK, a collector's item with collector's bills.

problems.title: M271 Timing Chain & Camshaft Adjuster Failure
problems.rootCause: The M271's timing chain tensioner can fail, leading to chain stretch and potentially catastrophic valve damage. Camshaft adjuster wear is also common — a rattling noise on cold start is the classic early warning sign. Oil leaks from the camshaft solenoid are another known issue.
problems.recommendation: On any M271-powered R172, listen for a rattling noise on cold start — if you hear it, get the timing chain inspected immediately. A £800–£1,500 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows a skipped chain. The M274 in the facelift SLC is a much safer bet.

cta.label: Compare M271 engine prices for your R172 SLK 200/250 →

problems.title: M276 V6 Timing Chain Wear
problems.rootCause: The M276 V6 can suffer from timing chain wear, particularly if oil changes have been neglected or the wrong oil has been used. A rattling noise on cold start is the classic symptom.
problems.recommendation: On any M276-powered R172 over 80,000 miles, have the timing chain inspected at every service — a £1,000–£1,800 repair is far cheaper than the £4,000–£6,500 replacement that follows a skipped chain. Use only Mercedes-spec oil to minimise wear.

cta.label: Compare M276 engine prices for your R172 SLK 350 →

problems.title: R172 Vario-Roof Mechanism Failure
problems.typicalFailureMileage: Any mileage, more common with age.
problems.rootCause: The R172's retractable hardtop (Vario-roof) is a complex electro-hydraulic system. The hydraulic rams can leak, the microswitches can fail, and the wiring loom can chafe. The roof can get stuck halfway or refuse to operate — a common and expensive problem on higher-mileage examples.
problems.recommendation: Have the Vario-roof mechanism inspected regularly — a £150–£400 inspection is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,000+ replacement that follows a complete system failure. Check for slow operation or unusual noises as early warning signs. This is the single most common non-engine issue on the R172.

problems.title: OM651 Injector Seal Failure
problems.rootCause: The OM651's injector seals can fail, allowing combustion gases to leak past — this creates carbon buildup and eventually causes injector seizure and cylinder damage. A diesel smell in the cabin or rough idle are classic symptoms.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rough idle or diesel smell, get the injector seals inspected immediately — a £400–£800 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,000–£5,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare OM651 engine prices for your R172 SLK 250 CDI →

problems.title: M152 V8 Camshaft Adjuster Wear
problems.rootCause: The M152 V8's camshaft adjusters can wear, causing a rattling noise on cold start and eventually leading to timing issues. This is a known issue on the M152 and related M157 engines.
problems.recommendation: If you own an SLK 55 AMG, listen for a rattling noise on cold start — if you hear it, get the camshaft adjusters inspected immediately. A £1,500–£2,500 repair is far cheaper than the £7,500–£12,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. The M152 is the last naturally aspirated V8 SLK — treat it with the respect it deserves.

cta.label: Compare M152 engine prices for your R172 SLK 55 AMG →

replacementCosts.h2: R172 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — R172 roadster shares its W204-derived engine bay with the C-Class, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. AMG costs reflect AMG-specific parts and specialist labour requirements.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are R172 roadster only. Pre-facelift models (2011–2015) are badged SLK; facelift models (2015–2020) are badged SLC. The R172 was the last SLK/SLC generation — discontinued after the 2020 model year.
